title: African urban forms: mapping resilience to climate change
markdown:
By **Jorge Patiño**, _Sahel and West Africa Club, OECD_, [@cocovski] (https://twitter.com/cocovski), 2024

**_Topic:**_ Climate, Urbanization, Sustainability 

**_Keywords:**_ Africa; Urban form; Sustainability; Resilience; Sprawl; Elongation

**_Africapolis data_**: GIS, agglomeration data


_***Original articles:**_    
Anderson, B., R. Prieto-Curiel and J. Patiño (2023). “City Shapes and Climate Change in Africa.“ West African Papers, No. 40, OECD Publishing, Paris. https://doi.org/10.1787/24142026. 

Prieto-Curiel, Rafael, Jorge E Patino, and Brilé Anderson. “Scaling of the Morphology of African Cities.“ PNAS 120, no. 9 (2023): e2214254120. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2214254120.

Patino, Jorge E., Rafael Prieto-Curiel, and Brilé Anderson. “Análisis de problemáticas urbanas a escala continental basado en datos abiertos: espacios verdes, forma urbana y sostenibilidad futura de las ciudades en África.” Territorios 49, no. 49-Esp. (October 24, 2023). https://doi.org/10.12804/revistas.urosario.edu.co/territorios/a.12775.

**_Abstract:**_

_Africa is experiencing a significant urban and climate transition. This analysis uses new methods and data to assess compactness and urban form attributes across 5,625 urban areas. It finds that very large cities (over 4 million people) are more compact than smaller and intermediate cities. Less compact cities typically have smaller buildings, less efficient land use, and larger intra-city distances, impacting sustainability and livability negatively. These cities face challenges like higher energy consumption for transport, less walkability, and increased air pollution. While compactness may reduce green space, it also offers opportunities for resilience planning. The report suggests targeted actions for improving urban sustainability and monitoring progress._ 

The story of compactness in African urban agglomerations is more complex than is often espoused, as are its implications for sustainability and resilience. As Africa undergoes unprecedented urbanisation and climate change, these transitions coincide with the unparalleled availability of open-source data. Yet, the nature of compactness in African urban agglomerations today is largely unknown beyond platitudes or a handful of one-off exercises in large metropolises. This [analysis](https://doi.org/10.1787/24142026) leverages the Africapolis database by combining it with the buildings' footprints from [Google Open Buildings](https://sites.research.google/open-buildings/) and the [OpenStreetMap](www.openstreetmap.org) street networks to calculate multiple indicators of urban form for 5,625 urban agglomerations across Africa. The indicators are available online on OECD/SWAC’s [Mapping Territorial Transformations in Africa](https://mapping-africa-transformations.org/climate-urbanform/) platform (MAPTA), offering an opportunity for actors to design evidence-based policies to inform urban planning.

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UF_0_EN.png)

######*Maps showing the degree of sprawl (top) and degree of elongation (bottom) in African urban agglomerations (explore the interactive map on [MAPTA](https://mapping-africa-transformations.org/climate-urbanform/)).* 

##### Measuring compactness

The maps above show the two indicators developed to capture urban compactness: the degree of sprawl and the degree of elongation. These were complemented with some metrics based on the buildings' footprint size, and the road network configuration from [OpenStreetMap](openstreetmap.org). These two measures encapsulate the morphological nature of compactness (see figure below), using the distances between buildings, instead of features such as mixed land-use or population densities. Sprawl measures the space between buildings (which is weighted to be comparable between cities). Higher values (above 1) signal more “empty” space or greater sprawl between buildings, while lower values (below 1) mean less distance between buildings and more compression. Elongation measures the extent to which the shape of the city departs from being a circle (weighted to be comparable between cities). A circle is the least elongated urban form possible (value of 1), a more elliptical shape increases distances between buildings in the city, while the most elongated shape is a line (which results in higher values). A compact city is rounder, with the least sprawl feasible (given terrain and needs – e.g. open spaces and streets). A stylised version is shown in the bottom-left quadrant in the figure below.

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UF_1_EN.png)

######*How sprawl and elongation relate to compactness. Top: The y-axis visualises a configuration of buildings for sprawl, while the x-axis visualises a configuration of buildings for elongation. Bottom: Examples of urban footprints and their degrees of elongation and sprawl in urban agglomerations with similar population sizes.* 

In Africa, very large cities (above 4 million inhabitants) are rounder and more compressed, showing less elongation and sprawl, compared to large cities (1 million to 4 million) and intermediate cities (50,000 to 1 million). Both sprawl and elongation increase with city size up to the large city category, but the evidence shows that very large cities fill in spaces between buildings and become more circular, thus reducing distances. The compactness of very large cities may result from more robust policy packages, as well as enhanced institutional capacities and resources. For example, Accra (Ghana), Abidjan (Côte d’Ivoire), Lagos (Nigeria), Johannesburg (South Africa), Kisumu (Kenya), Dar es Salaam (Tanzania), and Nairobi (Kenya) (cities above 4 million) have introduced or are in the process of introducing transit-oriented development strategies, which aim to create accessible, compact cities through mixed-use development and public transport.

##### Cost of less compact cities

Less compact urban agglomerations, characterised by high degrees of sprawl and elongation, share several traits. They have smaller buildings, relatively flat and low skylines, less dense city centres, and are more polycentric. Living in large cities presents significant downsides such as increased pollution, noise, and congestion, primarily due to the higher population density. One major issue is the increased distances between locations, leading to longer travel times to work, school, and essential services. This discourages active mobility like walking and cycling, raises transport costs, demands more energy and time, and increases emissions from private vehicles. Managing future energy demand from these uses is a critical sustainability priority for African urban areas. 

Understanding urban mobility requires extensive data, often unavailable for most African cities. Estimations based on average distances between buildings, however, can be a back-of-the-envelope calculation for future energy demand from transport, and show that intra-city distances increase with population size. Without sprawl, distances between buildings increase by 41% in cities with twice the population. Greater elongation and sprawl exacerbate this effect and significantly extend commuting distances and associated emissions. For instance, in Durban (South Africa), commuting distances double due to its elongated and sprawling urban form, which leads to higher emissions and more energy demand from transport.  

Longer distances ultimately lower accessibility, i.e. reducing access to services and opportunities within reasonable travel times. As the degree of sprawl increases, the number of street intersections per square kilometre decreases (street blocks become longer), limiting walkability and hence accessibility (see figure below that shows the number of street intersections per square kilometre and the degree of sprawl of thousands of African urban agglomerations). This is problematic since walking is the dominant means of transport for most residents in African urban agglomerations. If cities have lower accessibility, there is a risk of locking in unsustainable transport modes, such as private vehicles, instead of low-carbon modes, such as walking, cycling or public transport, which in turn leads to higher pollution and associated negative effects on health.

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_UF_2_EN.png)

######*Street intersection density vs sprawl. More sprawl makes cities less walkable.* 

Spatial data, combined with easily interpretable indicators, can help public authorities plan, manage, and guide urban development to meet the combined challenges of rapid urbanisation and climate change. City authorities, planners and stakeholders can leverage the increasing availability of open geospatial datasets and tools to support decision-making (with caution, as explained in this [paper](https://doi.org/10.1017/dap.2024.11)) and monitor progress towards more sustainable and climate-resilient cities.  



*The views, conclusions and materials of external contributors do not represent the views of OECD/SWAC or its Members.*

####*Author’s biography* 

![img](/images/tempimages/Patino_bio_rect.png)

*[Jorge Patiño](mailto:jorge.patino@oecd.org) holds an M.Sc. in Earth Sciences and a Ph.D. in Remote Sensing and GIS. He leads the Territorial Transformations Lab at OECD/SWAC, where he focuses on leveraging open geospatial data to enhance climate resilience and sustainability, with particular emphasis on the role of urban green spaces.*
